title: Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ERD)
content:
### Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ERD)

An ERD is a visual representation of entities (tables), their attributes (columns), and the relationships between them.

---

### Components of an ERD

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Entity | Rectangle | A table or object (e.g., Customer) |
| Attribute | Ellipse | A column property (e.g., name) |
| Primary Key | Underlined attribute | Uniquely identifies each row |
| Foreign Key | Dashed underline | References another entity's PK |
| Relationship | Diamond | How entities relate |

### Relationship Cardinality

| Notation | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| | | (one) | Exactly one |
| |< (many) | Zero or many |
| || (exactly one) | Exactly one (mandatory) |
| |o (optional) | Zero or one |

Crow's foot notation:

```
Customer 1 -----< N Order
  (one customer has many orders)

Employee N >----- 1 Department
  (many employees belong to one department)
```

### Types of Relationships

| Relationship | Example |
|-------------|---------|
| One-to-One (1:1) | Employee <-> EmployeeDetails |
| One-to-Many (1:M) | Department -> Employees |
| Many-to-Many (M:M) | Students <-> Courses (via Enrollment) |

### Converting ERD to Tables

```sql
-- 1:M relationship: Department (1) -> Employees (M)
CREATE TABLE departments (
    dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE employees (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(100),
    dept_id INT REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);

-- M:M relationship: Students M:M Courses via Enrollments
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
    student_id INT REFERENCES students(id),
    course_id INT REFERENCES courses(id),
    grade VARCHAR(2),
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id)
);
```

> **Key Point:** ERDs are a planning tool used before implementing the database schema. They help visualize relationships and identify design issues early.

quiz.question: What does a rectangle represent in an ERD?
How is a Many-to-Many relationship implemented in SQL?
